Tallying lost money through farm loophole

Paula Lavigne The Dallas Morning News February 2005
Business & EconomyAgriculture & Land

Explores how property owners, including major corporations, exploit a farm tax loophole to save millions annually at the expense of city, school, and county tax revenues.

Right across the street from our office building is a field. Every once in a while, we'll notice farm equipment rolling onto the property, tending to the growing rows of various crops. It's prime real estate, almost 110 acres near the intersection of two major highways. The owners are members of one of Dallas' most notable old money families who, among other pursuits, are land developers. They are not farmers.

Yet, by leasing their land to a farmer who reaps a nominal harvest, they get an agriculture tax break that saves them about $545,000 each year in taxes. Their savings is lost money for cities, schools, and county governments. Last year, two Morning News county reporters and I decided to investigate just how much tax money schools, cities, and counties lost through these agricultural exemptions. Our investigation revealed that a significant portion of the agricultural exemptions were enjoyed by non-farming enterprises, amounting to millions in lost local revenue.
